addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

## claude.ai Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Use this server whenever the user wants to create, generate, edit, implement, or sync any design, UI, screen, component, mockup, or visual — in Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or Figma Slides — and whenever the user mentions Figma or provides a figma.com URL.

This server bridges code and design in both directions, and supports designing from scratch using existing design systems and codebases.

CAPABILITIES:
- Read designs FROM Figma into code (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Write designs INTO Figma from code, intent, or existing components (use_figma, generate_figma_design, create_new_file, upload_assets)
- Bridge code and design via Code Connect (get_code_connect_map, add_code_connect_map)
- Create diagrams and FigJam content (generate_diagram, get_figjam)

WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user wants to create, mock up, or generate any UI, screen, component, or design — even if Figma isn't named
- The user wants to implement a design as code (design-to-code)
- The user wants to push a page, view, or component into Figma (code-to-design)
- The user wants to update, sync, or edit an existing Figma file
- The user shares a figma.com URL
- The user wants to build or extend a design system, design tokens, or component library
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

SKILLS (Prefer the skills shipped with the Figma plugin on the agent being used. If no Figma plugin is installed, use the skills served by the Figma MCP resource):
- /figma-use — MANDATORY before calling use_figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-use/SKILL.md

content.text:
# Gap-fill live retrieval — precise map

The gap-fill path is **the OUTLINE AGENT's `search_more_evidence` tool**, and it lives entirely in `src/polaris_graph/outline/outline_agent.py` + `src/polaris_graph/outline/_fold_in.py`. It is **not** in `verified_compose.py` (that file has zero retrieval; grep for `run_live_retrieval` returns no hits there). `weighted_enrichment.py` is a red herring — it re-routes *existing* unbound corpus rows, it never fetches.

---

## 1. Where gap-fill is triggered

**Entry seam** (the only place the agent loop is reachable from compose):
- `src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:9929-9941` — `generate_multi_section_report` calls `run_outline_agent_or_legacy(...)` inside the `else:` branch of the planner/legacy split.
- `src/polaris_graph/outline/outline_agent.py:2233-2451` — `run_outline_agent_or_legacy`: seeds via `_call_outline` (line 2250), builds `OutlineWorkspace` (2343), constructs `OutlineAgent` (2359), runs `agent.run()` under `asyncio.wait_for` (2378).

**The fetch itself:**
- `outline_agent.py:669-807` — `_tool_search_more_evidence(...)`. Derives a one-line query with an LLM (703-722), status-leak guard (734), then the FRESH fetch:
  ```python
  raw_result = await asyncio.to_thread(
      run_live_retrieval,
      research_question=workspace.research_question,
      amplified_queries=[query],
      domain=domain,
      anchor_seed=False,
      retrieval_deadline_monotonic=retrieval_deadline_monotonic,
  )                                            # outline_agent.py:756-763
  ```
- Rows are folded into the pool by `fold_in_fetched_rows` (`outline_agent.py:778-781` → `_fold_in.py:150-192`): url-dedup → `_offset_renumber` (`ev_{offset+i:03d}`, hard collision assert) → `_stamp_and_delete` → `workspace.ev_store[row["evidence_id"]] = row`.
- New rows are pushed back into the caller's `evidence` list at `outline_agent.py:2432-2434`:
  ```python
  for eid, row in final_ws.ev_store.items():
      if eid and eid not in ev_ids_before:
          evidence.append(row)
  ```
- Auto-assignment of the fetched rows into a section happens in `_execute` at `outline_agent.py:1758-1875` (reassign / add / remap-or-UNFILLED).

**Driver:** `OutlineAgent.run()` at `outline_agent.py:1905-1952` — seed checklist → `while turn < max_turns and elapsed < wall` → `_decide()` → `_execute()`.

---

## 2. EXACT gating conditions / caps

**A. Master seat**
- `PG_OUTLINE_AGENT` (default **OFF**) — `outline_agent.py:98-101`, checked at `2241`. OFF ⇒ pure pass-through to `_call_outline`, **zero** gap-fill. Set to `1` by `scripts/_run_16way_s3gear329.sh:15` and `scripts/compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py:483`.
- `outline_agent.py:2256-2258` — `if not parse_result.plans:` → "seed produced zero plans — skipping agent loop (fail-open)" ⇒ zero gap-fill.

**B. Hard budget caps (the only numeric caps on how many fetches can happen)**
- `PG_OUTLINE_AGENT_MAX_TURNS`, default **24** (`outline_agent.py:106`, `174`). Loop condition `while ws.turn < self.max_turns` (line 1910). **At most ONE `search_more_evidence` per turn** (`_decide` picks exactly one action) ⇒ **max 24 fetch calls per run**.
- `PG_OUTLINE_AGENT_WALL_SECONDS`, default **900** (`outline_agent.py:108`, `178`); also the in-flight retrieval deadline (`retrieval_deadline_monotonic = workspace.started_monotonic + wall`, `1005-1009`).
- `PG_OUTLINE_AGENT_RUN_TIMEOUT_GRACE_SECONDS`, default **600** (`153`, used at `2380-2383`).
- `PG_OUTLINE_GAP_RETRIES_PER_ASPECT`, default **2** (`107`, `197-199`) — `GapLedger._max_retries`; `mark_retry_or_unfilled` flips a todo to UNFILLED at the cap (`416-422`). UNFILLED todos are never re-selected by `next_pending()` (`403-407`).
- `PG_OUTLINE_DECIDE_WALL_SECONDS` (660), `PG_OUTLINE_DECIDE_MAX_TOKENS` (131072), `PG_OUTLINE_CHECKLIST_MAX_TOKENS` (131072), `PG_OUTLINE_QUERY_DERIVE_MAX_TOKENS` (131072) — `1246-1252`, `1378-1379`, `698-700`. A decide failure **breaks the loop** (`1914-1917`), a checklist failure **fails open to "no deficiencies"** (`1421-1427`).
- There is **no cap on rows added per fetch**. `run_live_retrieval` returns whatever it returns; every survivor of the fold-in screen is inserted. Observed in `logs/outline_postgate.log:1421`: a single call kept **37** rows.

**C. The predicate that actually decides IF gap-fill runs — and this is the crux**

**Root cause found, and it's one line.** Across every log this system has ever produced: **28 `coverage` gaps, 1 `numeric_rows`, and ZERO `density`.** The density arm — the only one that asks *"is this section backed by too few sources?"* — has never fired once.

Why: line 1454 runs `_quote_is_grounded(quote, question_norm)` on **every** line *before* `kind` is parsed at 1458. That gate demands a verbatim quote **from the question** naming the missing facet. But a section is thin regardless of whether the question happens to contain words describing its thinness — so a density gap can almost never produce a valid question-quote, and gets dropped as "ungrounded". The precision gate built for the *coverage* arm silently kills the *depth* arm.

That is the depth ceiling: we stop as soon as every question-named facet has *a* section, however thin. Let me gather the remaining evidence before proposing anything.

The picture is now complete, and the section shape proves it:

| rows | section |
|---|---|
| 78 | Measuring Occupational Exposure to Generative AI |
| 51 | Productivity Enhancement and Task Augmentation |
| **17** | **Global Divides and Sectoral Heterogeneity** |
| **9** | **Policy Responses and Institutional Frameworks** |
| **6** | **Cross-Study Synthesis and Contradictions** |

The question is three sentences ("...as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution... affecting various industries... high-quality English-language journal articles"). Every facet it *names* has a section, so the coverage arm returns NONE and the run finishes at turn 8 with 16 turns of retrieval budget unspent — while Policy sits on 9 sources and Cross-Study on 6. A human reviewer would say "your policy section is thin, go get more." The checklist structurally **cannot** say that, because "policy responses" is not a verbatim substring of the question.

Rank3's lesson was that Fable refuted my diagnosis and saved a bad change. So I gate the diagnosis *before* writing code.

input.prompt:
You are the ADVERSARIAL GATE for an autonomous research-report system. Your job is to REFUTE, not to agree. A prior gate of yours already saved this project by refuting a confident-but-wrong diagnosis; do that again if it is warranted. Read the real code and real artifacts — do not reason from my summary alone.

REPO: /home/polaris/wt/flywheel (branch flywheel-v1). Python. Read-only: DO NOT EDIT ANY FILE.

## CONTEXT
This system writes literature reviews. Known quality gap: our reports are ~4k words; frontier competitors (ChatGPT/Gemini Deep Research) produce 10-22k. We just landed a topic judge (Rank5) that deletes genuinely off-topic rows from the evidence pool (507/944 deleted, validated: 97% row-level verdict stability across independently shuffled passes; zero seminal papers lost). The pool went 944 -> ~437 rows. The report got shorter but truer. So the remaining honest way to regain LENGTH is FRESH ON-TOPIC EVIDENCE (more retrieval), not padding.

The outline agent (src/polaris_graph/outline/outline_agent.py) has a `search_more_evidence` tool = live gap-fill retrieval. Budget: max 24 turns (PG_OUTLINE_AGENT_MAX_TURNS), one fetch per turn, 900s wall.

## MY DIAGNOSIS (attack it)
The gap-fill loop is driven by `_run_checklist` (outline_agent.py ~1277-1542). It asks the LLM for deficiencies of three kinds: `coverage | density | numeric_rows`. Each returned line is `<section> :: <aspect> :: <kind> :: <verbatim quote from QUESTION>`.

CLAIM 1 (structural): at line ~1454 the code runs `_quote_is_grounded(quote, question_norm)` on EVERY line and drops it as "ungrounded" if the quote is not a literal substring (>= _MIN_GROUNDING_QUOTE_WORDS words) of the RESEARCH QUESTION. This runs BEFORE `kind` is parsed (line ~1458). Therefore a DENSITY deficiency ("this section is backed by too few sources") must also produce a verbatim quote from the question naming the missing facet — but thinness of a section is not a property the question's wording ever names. So the density arm is structurally near-unfireable.

CLAIM 2 (empirical): across every log the system has ever written (logs/*.log), the recorded gap kinds are 28 `coverage`, 1 `numeric_rows`, and ZERO `density`. The density arm has never fired once.

CLAIM 3 (consequence): retrieval therefore stops as soon as every facet LITERALLY NAMED in the question has some section, no matter how thin. Evidence: the Rank5 armed run (logs/rank5_armed2.log) logged `checklist[seed] ran: NONE (no grounded deficiencies)` and `finish_outline ACCEPTED at turn 8: pending=0, budget_remains=True` — 0 gap-fill fetches, 16 turns of retrieval budget unspent — while its outline (outputs/rank5_deleted_outline.json) has sections with 6, 9, and 17 rows next to sections with 78 and 51. The 3-sentence question is in /home/polaris/polaris_project/task72_prompt.txt. Note the dirty-pool control run (logs/rank4_judged.log) DID find gaps (`checklist[seed] named 2 gap(s)`) and fetched 3 times.

## MY PROPOSED FIX (Rank6) — attack this hardest
1. Parse `kind` BEFORE the grounding gate (pure reorder of the existing lines).
2. kind=`coverage`: keep the question-quote gate EXACTLY as today. Unchanged. It is a working anti-invention precision gate (it correctly kills hallucinated "Summary Table" sections — see the `dropped N ungrounded line(s)` log lines).
3. kind=`density`: change the grounding ANCHOR (not remove it). A density line must quote verbatim from the NAMED SECTION'S OWN title+focus (which is itself already question-derived and already an approved part of the outline) rather than from the question. Rationale: a density gap by definition asks for MORE OF AN ALREADY-APPROVED TOPIC, so it cannot introduce new subject matter; the anti-invention property is preserved, just anchored to the right text.
4. Add a MECHANICAL precision guard the prompt currently only states in prose: accept a density line only if the named section's actual ev_id count is genuinely low relative to the outline (e.g. below median / below a floor), so the model cannot claim the 78-row section is thin.
5. Bound the blast radius: cap density todos per checklist round (K thinnest sections); coverage gaps served before density gaps; existing PG_OUTLINE_GAP_RETRIES_PER_ASPECT=2 -> UNFILLED still applies. New lever ships behind a DEFAULT-OFF env kill-switch (PG_OUTLINE_DENSITY_GAPS) for a clean A/B.

SAFETY ARGUMENT I AM RELYING ON (test it): retrieving more rows is now SAFE in a way it wasn't before, because gap-fill rows are folded in through `src/polaris_graph/outline/_fold_in.py` which applies the SAME content-integrity junk detector AND the SAME (now-armed, now-validated) topic judge as the corpus gate. So fresh alien/chrome rows get screened at fold-in.

## YOUR TASK
Verify every claim against the actual code and artifacts. Then adversarially attack:
(a) Is CLAIM 1 actually true, or is there some other path by which a density gap could fire (a different producer, a fallback, a remap, _screen_status_lines behavior)? Is the density arm truly dead, or merely rare? Is `numeric_rows`'s single firing a counterexample that breaks my reasoning?
(b) Is my causal story for `checklist[seed] ran: NONE` right? Could the NONE instead be caused by something else entirely (seed outline shape, a different prompt path, the LLM's Rule-5 anti-redundancy nudge, an exception, a screen)? What OTHER explanation fits the evidence better? Note the dirty run found gaps and the clean run didn't — explain that under YOUR best theory, not mine.
(c) Will my fix actually produce DEPTH, or will it produce a retrieval treadmill / junk influx / turn starvation / infinite thin-section loop? What breaks?
(d) Is the section-title+focus anchor a real anti-invention gate, or is it trivially gameable (the focus text is model-generated — is it a weaker gate than I claim, and does that matter)?
(e) Is the "_fold_in screens fetched rows" safety argument TRUE? Read _fold_in.py and verify the junk + topic screens actually run on gap-fill rows, with what defaults, and whether they can be silently inert (this project has a history of gates that were dead code or no-ops).
(f) What is the SINGLE highest-value thing I am missing? Is there a better lever for the 4k-vs-20k depth gap than the density arm? Be concrete. Consider: is the outline agent even the binding constraint on final word count, or is the COMPOSE/generation stage the real ceiling (i.e. would more evidence rows even become more words)? That last question may be the most important one — check how section word budgets are actually determined, because if compose caps words independently of evidence, my whole Rank6 is worthless and you must tell me so.

content.text:
Fable mis-cited the file (it's `WHEEL_PROGRESS.md:259-261`, not the compose script), but the **substance is real and it's in this project's own record**:

> `(1) THIN body (2141 words vs step3 3230) ... kept_fraction=0.35 < 0.40 retry) -> 102 dropped sentences (step3=64), 77 verified (step3=91)`

So compose is the binding ceiling, and more rows per section made prose *thinner* by drowning strict_verify. Rank6-as-designed is dead. **But this reframes something Fable missed**, and it's the most important thing on the board:

**Every one of those compose measurements was taken on the DIRTY corpus — the one that was ~50% alien.** The writer's menu contained job listings, paralegal reading lists and Cloudflare captchas; it wrote sentences from them; strict_verify correctly killed them. That *is* `kept_fraction=0.35`. Rank5 just removed exactly those rows — and **we have never once composed a report from the clean pool.** Rank5 stopped at outline-only.

So the next step isn't a code change at all. It's the missing measurement.

It's default-OFF and the driver never sets it.

**2. Rank6 was refuted, and the gate caught an error in my own evidence.** I diagnosed the outline agent's "density" gap arm as dead code and was about to fix it. Fable showed my key statistic was an artifact: the accepted-gap log line never records the gap *kind*, so my "zero density gaps ever" was counting **rejected** lines, not accepted ones. The fix I'd have shipped would have been a no-op. That's the second time this gate has saved a wasted change.

**3. The real finding — the depth gap was never about evidence.** The ~4k-word ceiling is hard-coded: the module docstring literally promises *"1500-3000-word reports"*, and the section writer is told *"Target 10-18 sentences"* regardless of how much evidence it holds. Eleven sections × 10–18 sentences ≈ our exact word count. More evidence cannot make the report longer — and this project already measured that pushing more rows in makes it *shorter*, by drowning strict_verify.

**4. But that measurement is confounded, and I'm testing it now.** Every compose measurement in this repo's history was taken on the corpus that was ~50% alien — the writer's menu held job listings, paralegal reading lists and Cloudflare captchas, it wrote sentences from them, and strict_verify correctly killed them. **That is what `kept_fraction=0.35` is.** Rank5 removed precisely those rows, and we have never once composed a report from the clean pool. A matched A/B (identical commit, one env var apart) is running to settle whether compose is a hard architectural ceiling or was simply being fed garbage.

If the clean pool lifts `kept_fraction`, the same fixed prompt yields more verified words for free, and the levers above (`PG_VERIFIED_COMPOSE`'s per-basket fan-out, evidence-scaled sentence targets) become the honest path to closing the 4k→20k gap.
